questions.question: A 12 kg crate is pushed across a horizontal floor with a force of 45 N. If the crate moves at a constant velocity, what is the magnitude of the kinetic friction acting on it?
questions.explanation: At constant velocity, net force is zero, so kinetic friction equals the applied force: 45 N.

questions.question: Which of the following changes would increase the coefficient of kinetic friction between two surfaces?
questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction depends on the materials in contact. Normal force, surface area, and velocity affect the friction force but not the coefficient itself.

questions.question: An object remains at rest on a rough horizontal surface even though a horizontal force of 15 N is applied. What is the magnitude of the static friction force?
questions.explanation: Static friction matches the applied force to keep the object at rest, up to its maximum. Since the object doesn't move, static friction equals 15 N.

questions.question: A wooden block with a mass of 4 kg is on a table. If the coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.3, what is the force of friction acting on the block as it slides? (Use g = 9.8 m/s²)
If the net force acting on a moving object is suddenly reduced to zero, what happens to the object's motion?
options: It gradually slows to a stop
It moves at a constant velocity
questions.explanation: According to Newton's First Law, if net force is zero, an object in motion stays in motion at constant velocity.

questions.question: Which type of friction is generally the strongest for the same two surfaces?
questions.explanation: Static friction is typically the strongest because surfaces haven't broken free from each other yet. It takes more force to start motion than to maintain it.

questions.question: A force of 60 N is applied to a 10 kg sled on ice, where friction is negligible. What is the acceleration of the sled?
questions.explanation: Using F = ma: a = F/m = 60 N / 10 kg = 6 m/s².

questions.question: How does the force of friction change if the mass of a sliding object is doubled?
questions.explanation: Friction force f = μF_N = μmg. Doubling mass doubles the normal force, so friction doubles.

questions.question: In a free-body diagram of a block being pulled to the right across a floor, in which direction should the arrow for friction be drawn?
questions.explanation: Friction always opposes motion. If the block moves right, kinetic friction acts to the left.

questions.question: What happens to the acceleration of an object if the applied force is doubled while the friction force remains constant?
questions.explanation: Doubling applied force increases net force (F_net = F_applied - F_friction), so acceleration increases. It does not double exactly unless friction is zero.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered friction and net force concepts!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Solid understanding, but review static vs. kinetic friction and Newton's First Law.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on friction calculations (f = μF_N) and Newton's Laws of motion.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: types of friction, friction calculations, and how net force affects motion.
